text.title: Solo Alert Routing — Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules that keep one human's attention reliable. If alarm fatigue sets in, the entire system is worse than no alerting.

rule.statement: Noise reduction happens at the SOURCE (Sentry filter rules, Plausible cooldown, mail-bounce thresholds), not downstream. A rule that lets 100 events through and then filters in the digest layer is forbidden.

rationale.source: alerting-pipeline best practice; Google SRE Workbook

rule.rationale: Downstream filtering still costs the source's quota, still records in logs, still consumes the operator's brain when scrolling. Source filtering eliminates the noise before it enters the pipeline at all.

rule.statement: Default delivery is hourly digest, not per-event. Only specific high-severity classes (uptime down, payment 5xx burst, security event) bypass digest. New event types start in digest until proven worth waking the operator for.

rationale.source: cognitive load research; field experience with solo operators

rule.rationale: A solo operator can absorb ~5 interruptions/day before quality drops. Digest collapses 50 events into one readable summary; per-event delivery saturates attention by 10am.

rule.statement: Quiet hours (22:00-08:00 operator local time, weekends configurable) HARD-cut all alert delivery except a small allow-list (uptime down ≥10min, error-rate spike ≥100/min, security event). Marketing-soft alerts (Plausible traffic anomaly) NEVER break quiet hours.

rationale.source: Stanford sleep research; sustainable-pace agile principle

rule.rationale: A woken operator is impaired for the following workday and tends to over-react. The hard cut is the difference between solo operation being sustainable and burning out within 6 months. The allow-list captures only true-business-impact events.

rule.statement: There is exactly ONE high-priority channel the operator commits to monitor (a named Telegram chat, a specific email folder, or an SMS number). All high-severity alerts route there. Splitting across channels is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Two channels mean checking both; three means checking none reliably. The single-channel rule forces honest prioritisation: only events worth interrupting that channel pass the gate.

rule.statement: Every alert has a defined severity (S1 down, S2 degraded, S3 anomaly, S4 informational) AND a delivery rule per severity. Severity is set at the source; the digest pipeline does not invent severity.
rule.rationale: Without explicit severity, the operator re-evaluates every incoming alert. With severity, the pipeline routes deterministically and the operator's energy goes to response, not triage.

section.title: Default severity-to-delivery matrix
section.code:
S1 (production down, payment 5xx burst, security incident, data loss risk): immediate page to single channel, breaks quiet hours, retry every 10 min until acknowledged.
S2 (degraded perf, error-rate spike on non-critical path, expired cert in 7 days): immediate notification during work hours (08:00-22:00), digest only at night.
S3 (Plausible anomaly, new error pattern, mail bounce uptick, slow query): hourly digest only. Never wakes the operator.
S4 (info, deploy success, weekly reports): daily 09:00 digest.

example.title: Quiet-hours hard cut in action
example.code: 03:14 — Sentry detects 8 errors/min on the export endpoint. Severity tagged S3. 03:14 — Pipeline checks quiet hours: 22:00-08:00 → S3 routed to next 09:00 digest only. 03:14 — Pipeline checks allow-list: S3 not on the list → does not break quiet hours. 03:18 — Uptime monitor reports faion.net down. Severity tagged S1. 03:18 — Pipeline checks allow-list: S1 down on list → pages operator immediately. Outcome: operator wakes once for the real incident; the export errors are investigated calmly at 09:30 with coffee.
example.note: r1-r5 all in play; the operator's sleep is preserved AND the real incident is caught.

example.title: The pager-for-everything anti-pattern
example.code: Sentry: per-event email on every error. Plausible: per-threshold email on every anomaly. Uptime: SMS on every flap (5 false alerts/day from flaky network). Outcome: operator turns off all notifications by month 2; misses the real Sentry data-loss event at month 3.
example.note: r1 (no source filtering), r2 (no digest), r3 (no quiet hours), r4 (multi-channel mess), r5 (no severity). The system is worse than no alerting.
